instruction: Explain and implement Rust's memory safety guarantees without garbage collection. Show how ownership, borrowing, and lifetimes prevent use-after-free, double-free, and data races at compile time.
output:
<think>
Rust achieves memory safety without a GC through three key mechanisms: ownership (each value has exactly one owner), borrowing (references that don't own the data), and lifetimes (compiler-enforced reference validity).

Ownership rules: (1) each value has one owner, (2) when the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped, (3) ownership can be moved (transferred) but not copied (unless the type implements Copy).

Borrowing rules: (1) you can have either one mutable reference OR any number of immutable references at a time, (2) references must always be valid (no dangling pointers). This is enforced at compile time by the borrow checker.

Lifetimes: annotations that tell the compiler how long references are valid. Usually inferred (lifetime elision), but sometimes explicit annotation is needed, especially in function signatures and structs that hold references.

These prevent:
- Use-after-free: ownership ensures data isn't freed while references exist
- Double-free: single ownership means only one Drop call
- Data races: borrow checker prevents simultaneous mutable and immutable access
- Dangling pointers: lifetime checking ensures references don't outlive data

I'll show concrete examples of what the compiler catches and how to structure code that the borrow checker accepts.
</think>

```rust
// === Ownership: Prevents double-free ===

fn ownership_demo() {
    let s1 = String::from("hello"); // s1 owns the string
    let s2 = s1;                     // ownership MOVED to s2
    // println!("{}", s1);           // COMPILE ERROR: s1 is no longer valid
    println!("{}", s2);              // OK: s2 owns it now
} // s2 dropped here — exactly ONE free, no double-free


// === Borrowing: Prevents data races ===

fn borrowing_demo() {
    let mut data = vec![1, 2, 3];
    
    // Multiple immutable borrows: OK
    let r1 = &data;
    let r2 = &data;
    println!("{:?} {:?}", r1, r2);
    
    // Mutable borrow: OK (r1, r2 no longer used after this point)
    let r3 = &mut data;
    r3.push(4);
    println!("{:?}", r3);
    
    // WOULD NOT COMPILE:
    // let r4 = &data;      // immutable borrow
    // let r5 = &mut data;  // mutable borrow while immutable exists
    // println!("{:?}", r4); // ERROR: can't have both
}


// === Lifetimes: Prevents use-after-free ===

// Explicit lifetime: returned reference must live as long as both inputs
fn longest<'a>(x: &'a str, y: &'a str) -> &'a str {
    if x.len() > y.len() { x } else { y }
}

fn lifetime_demo() {
    let result;
    let string1 = String::from("long string");
    {
        let string2 = String::from("xyz");
        result = longest(string1.as_str(), string2.as_str());
        println!("Longest: {}", result); // OK: both strings alive
    }
    // Using result here would be ERROR if it referenced string2
    // The borrow checker catches this at compile time
}


// === Struct with lifetime: reference must outlive the struct ===

struct TextExcerpt<'a> {
    content: &'a str,  // borrows from some string
}

impl<'a> TextExcerpt<'a> {
    fn new(text: &'a str) -> Self {
        let first_sentence = text.split('.').next().unwrap_or(text);
        TextExcerpt { content: first_sentence }
    }
    
    fn display(&self) {
        println!("Excerpt: {}", self.content);
    }
}


// === Safe interior mutability with RefCell (runtime borrow checking) ===

use std::cell::RefCell;
use std::rc::Rc;

struct SafeCache {
    data: RefCell<Vec<String>>,
}

impl SafeCache {
    fn new() -> Self {
        SafeCache { data: RefCell::new(Vec::new()) }
    }
    
    fn add(&self, item: String) {
        // Interior mutability: modify through shared reference
        // Panics at RUNTIME if borrow rules violated
        self.data.borrow_mut().push(item);
    }
    
    fn get_all(&self) -> Vec<String> {
        self.data.borrow().clone()
    }
}


// === Thread safety: Send + Sync traits ===

use std::sync::{Arc, Mutex};
use std::thread;

fn thread_safety_demo() {
    // Arc: atomic reference counting (shared ownership across threads)
    // Mutex: interior mutability with locking
    let counter = Arc::new(Mutex::new(0));
    let mut handles = vec![];
    
    for _ in 0..10 {
        let counter = Arc::clone(&counter);
        let handle = thread::spawn(move || {
            let mut num = counter.lock().unwrap();
            *num += 1;
            // Lock automatically released when `num` goes out of scope
        });
        handles.push(handle);
    }
    
    for handle in handles {
        handle.join().unwrap();
    }
    
    println!("Counter: {}", *counter.lock().unwrap()); // Always 10
}


fn main() {
    ownership_demo();
    borrowing_demo();
    lifetime_demo();
    
    let text = String::from("Rust is safe. Memory is managed.");
    let excerpt = TextExcerpt::new(&text);
    excerpt.display();
    
    let cache = SafeCache::new();
    cache.add("item1".to_string());
    cache.add("item2".to_string());
    println!("Cache: {:?}", cache.get_all());
    
    thread_safety_demo();
    
    println!("All safety demos completed successfully");
}
```

instruction: Write guidelines for safe usage of `unsafe` blocks in Rust. Demonstrate when unsafe is justified, how to minimize its scope, and how to build safe abstractions on top of unsafe foundations.
output:
<think>
Unsafe Rust unlocks five superpowers: dereference raw pointers, call unsafe functions, access mutable statics, implement unsafe traits, and access union fields. The key principle: unsafe doesn't turn off the borrow checker — it only allows these five specific operations. All other safety guarantees still apply.

When unsafe is justified:
1. FFI (calling C libraries) — inherently unsafe, no Rust guarantees
2. Performance-critical hot loops where bounds checking overhead matters
3. Low-level primitives (implementing Mutex, atomic operations)
4. Interacting with hardware (memory-mapped I/O)

The safe abstraction pattern: wrap unsafe code in a safe API. The unsafe block is the implementation detail; callers only see the safe interface. The module boundary acts as the trust boundary.

Key guidelines:
- Minimize unsafe scope to the smallest possible block
- Document the safety invariants as comments (// SAFETY: ...)
- Ensure the safe wrapper maintains all invariants
- Use #[deny(unsafe_op_in_unsafe_fn)] to force explicit unsafe blocks even inside unsafe functions
- Test extensively, including with Miri (Rust's undefined behavior detector)
- Prefer safe alternatives first (Vec instead of raw allocation, etc.)

I'll show several examples: raw pointer manipulation, FFI, building a safe abstraction over unsafe code, and common mistakes to avoid.
</think>

```rust
#![deny(unsafe_op_in_unsafe_fn)]  // Force explicit unsafe blocks in unsafe fns

use std::alloc::{alloc, dealloc, Layout};
use std::ptr;


// === Example 1: Safe abstraction over raw memory ===

/// A fixed-size buffer backed by raw allocation.
/// SAFETY CONTRACT: All public methods maintain these invariants:
/// - `ptr` always points to a valid allocation of `capacity` elements
/// - `len <= capacity` at all times
/// - Elements 0..len are initialized and valid
pub struct RawBuffer<T> {
    ptr: *mut T,
    len: usize,
    capacity: usize,
}

impl<T> RawBuffer<T> {
    pub fn with_capacity(capacity: usize) -> Self {
        assert!(capacity > 0, "capacity must be > 0");
        let layout = Layout::array::<T>(capacity).expect("layout overflow");
        
        // SAFETY: layout is non-zero (capacity > 0, T is not ZST assumed)
        let ptr = unsafe { alloc(layout) as *mut T };
        if ptr.is_null() {
            std::alloc::handle_alloc_error(layout);
        }
        
        RawBuffer { ptr, len: 0, capacity }
    }
    
    /// Push an element. Panics if at capacity.
    pub fn push(&mut self, value: T) {
        assert!(self.len < self.capacity, "buffer full");
        
        // SAFETY: len < capacity, so ptr.add(len) is within allocation.
        // We're writing to uninitialized memory (no drop of old value needed).
        unsafe {
            ptr::write(self.ptr.add(self.len), value);
        }
        self.len += 1;
    }
    
    /// Get reference to element at index. Returns None if out of bounds.
    pub fn get(&self, index: usize) -> Option<&T> {
        if index >= self.len {
            return None;
        }
        // SAFETY: index < len, and elements 0..len are initialized.
        Some(unsafe { &*self.ptr.add(index) })
    }
    
    pub fn len(&self) -> usize {
        self.len
    }
}

impl<T> Drop for RawBuffer<T> {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        // SAFETY: Drop all initialized elements, then free memory
        unsafe {
            for i in 0..self.len {
                ptr::drop_in_place(self.ptr.add(i));
            }
            let layout = Layout::array::<T>(self.capacity).unwrap();
            dealloc(self.ptr as *mut u8, layout);
        }
    }
}

// Send + Sync only if T is Send + Sync
// SAFETY: RawBuffer has exclusive ownership of its allocation
unsafe impl<T: Send> Send for RawBuffer<T> {}
unsafe impl<T: Sync> Sync for RawBuffer<T> {}


// === Example 2: Safe FFI wrapper ===

mod ffi_example {
    use std::ffi::{CStr, CString};
    use std::os::raw::c_char;
    
    // Hypothetical C function
    extern "C" {
        fn strlen(s: *const c_char) -> usize;
    }
    
    /// Safe wrapper around C's strlen.
    /// Panics if the input contains null bytes.
    pub fn safe_strlen(s: &str) -> usize {
        let c_string = CString::new(s)
            .expect("string contains null byte");
        
        // SAFETY: CString guarantees null-termination and valid pointer.
        // strlen only reads memory up to the null terminator.
        unsafe { strlen(c_string.as_ptr()) }
    }
}


// === Example 3: Common mistakes with unsafe ===

mod common_mistakes {
    /// BAD: Unsafe scope too wide
    pub fn bad_example(data: &[u8]) -> u8 {
        unsafe {
            // This entire block is unsafe, but only the
            // unchecked indexing needs it
            let len = data.len();
            let first = *data.get_unchecked(0);  // only this needs unsafe
            let processed = first.wrapping_add(1);  // safe operation
            processed
        }
    }
    
    /// GOOD: Minimal unsafe scope
    pub fn good_example(data: &[u8]) -> u8 {
        assert!(!data.is_empty(), "data must not be empty");
        
        // SAFETY: We just checked data is non-empty, so index 0 is valid
        let first = unsafe { *data.get_unchecked(0) };
        let processed = first.wrapping_add(1);  // safe, outside unsafe block
        processed
    }
}


fn main() {
    // RawBuffer: safe API, unsafe internals
    let mut buf = RawBuffer::with_capacity(10);
    buf.push(String::from("hello"));
    buf.push(String::from("world"));
    
    assert_eq!(buf.get(0).map(|s| s.as_str()), Some("hello"));
    assert_eq!(buf.get(1).map(|s| s.as_str()), Some("world"));
    assert_eq!(buf.get(2), None);  // bounds checked
    assert_eq!(buf.len(), 2);
    
    println!("RawBuffer works safely: {:?}", buf.get(0));
    // buf is dropped here — memory properly freed, strings dropped
    
    println!("All unsafe abstraction tests PASS");
}
```

instruction: Demonstrate FFI safety boundaries in Rust when calling C code and when exposing Rust functions to C. Show proper error handling, null pointer safety, and how to prevent undefined behavior at the boundary.
output:
<think>
FFI (Foreign Function Interface) is where Rust's safety guarantees meet the unsafe world of C. Every FFI call is inherently unsafe because Rust can't verify C code's behavior. The goal is to create the thinnest possible unsafe layer and wrap it in a safe Rust API.

Key safety concerns at FFI boundaries:
1. Null pointers: C functions may return null, Rust references can't be null
2. String handling: C strings are null-terminated, Rust strings are length-prefixed UTF-8
3. Memory ownership: who allocates, who frees? Mixing allocators is UB
4. Error codes: C uses int returns, Rust uses Result
5. Lifetime management: C doesn't track lifetimes, Rust does
6. Thread safety: C functions may not be thread-safe
7. Panics: Rust panics must NOT unwind across FFI boundaries (UB)

Best practices:
- Always validate C function return values before using them
- Convert between C types and Rust types at the boundary
- Use CStr/CString for string conversion
- Catch panics at FFI export boundaries with catch_unwind
- Document all safety requirements
- Use Option<NonNull<T>> instead of raw *mut T where possible
</think>

```rust
use std::ffi::{c_char, c_int, c_void, CStr, CString};
use std::ptr::{self, NonNull};
use std::panic::catch_unwind;


// === Calling C from Rust (safe wrappers) ===

// Raw C bindings — minimal, just the declarations
mod sys {
    use std::ffi::{c_char, c_int, c_void};
    
    extern "C" {
        pub fn open(path: *const c_char, flags: c_int) -> c_int;
        pub fn close(fd: c_int) -> c_int;
        pub fn malloc(size: usize) -> *mut c_void;
        pub fn free(ptr: *mut c_void);
        pub fn strerror(errnum: c_int) -> *const c_char;
    }
}


/// Safe wrapper for C's malloc/free.
/// Provides RAII: memory is freed when CBuffer is dropped.
pub struct CBuffer {
    ptr: NonNull<c_void>,  // NonNull guarantees non-null
    size: usize,
}

impl CBuffer {
    pub fn new(size: usize) -> Result<Self, String> {
        if size == 0 {
            return Err("Cannot allocate 0 bytes".into());
        }
        
        // SAFETY: malloc with non-zero size. We check for null return.
        let raw = unsafe { sys::malloc(size) };
        let ptr = NonNull::new(raw)
            .ok_or_else(|| "malloc returned null (out of memory)".to_string())?;
        
        Ok(CBuffer { ptr, size })
    }
    
    pub fn as_slice(&self) -> &[u8] {
        // SAFETY: ptr is valid for size bytes (from malloc), properly aligned for u8
        unsafe {
            std::slice::from_raw_parts(self.ptr.as_ptr() as *const u8, self.size)
        }
    }
}

impl Drop for CBuffer {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        // SAFETY: ptr was allocated by malloc, freed exactly once here
        unsafe { sys::free(self.ptr.as_ptr()) };
    }
}


/// Safe wrapper for C error messages.
fn get_error_message(errno: c_int) -> String {
    // SAFETY: strerror returns a valid static string pointer for valid errno
    let ptr = unsafe { sys::strerror(errno) };
    if ptr.is_null() {
        return format!("Unknown error {}", errno);
    }
    // SAFETY: ptr is non-null and points to a null-terminated C string
    let cstr = unsafe { CStr::from_ptr(ptr) };
    cstr.to_string_lossy().into_owned()
}


// === Exposing Rust to C (safe exports) ===

/// Opaque type for C consumers.
/// C code gets a pointer; only Rust code accesses internals.
pub struct ProcessorHandle {
    data: Vec<f64>,
    name: String,
}

/// Create a new processor. Returns null on failure.
/// 
/// # Safety
/// Caller must eventually call `processor_free` on the returned pointer.
#[no_mangle]
pub extern "C" fn processor_new(name: *const c_char) -> *mut ProcessorHandle {
    // Catch panics — unwinding across FFI is UB
    let result = catch_unwind(|| {
        if name.is_null() {
            return ptr::null_mut();
        }
        
        // SAFETY: caller guarantees name is a valid null-terminated string
        let name_str = unsafe { CStr::from_ptr(name) };
        let name_owned = match name_str.to_str() {
            Ok(s) => s.to_owned(),
            Err(_) => return ptr::null_mut(), // Invalid UTF-8
        };
        
        let handle = Box::new(ProcessorHandle {
            data: Vec::new(),
            name: name_owned,
        });
        
        Box::into_raw(handle)  // Transfer ownership to C
    });
    
    result.unwrap_or(ptr::null_mut())
}

/// Add a value to the processor.
/// Returns 0 on success, -1 on error.
#[no_mangle]
pub extern "C" fn processor_add(
    handle: *mut ProcessorHandle,
    value: f64,
) -> c_int {
    let result = catch_unwind(|| {
        if handle.is_null() {
            return -1;
        }
        // SAFETY: handle was created by processor_new and not yet freed
        let processor = unsafe { &mut *handle };
        processor.data.push(value);
        0
    });
    
    result.unwrap_or(-1)
}

/// Free processor. Safe to call with null (no-op).
#[no_mangle]
pub extern "C" fn processor_free(handle: *mut ProcessorHandle) {
    if handle.is_null() {
        return;
    }
    // SAFETY: handle was allocated by Box::into_raw in processor_new.
    // This takes back ownership and drops it.
    let _ = catch_unwind(|| {
        unsafe { drop(Box::from_raw(handle)) };
    });
}


fn main() {
    // Test CBuffer
    let buf = CBuffer::new(1024).expect("allocation failed");
    println!("Allocated {} bytes via C malloc", buf.as_slice().len());
    // Automatically freed on drop
    
    // Test FFI export functions
    let name = CString::new("test").unwrap();
    let handle = processor_new(name.as_ptr());
    assert!(!handle.is_null());
    
    assert_eq!(processor_add(handle, 1.0), 0);
    assert_eq!(processor_add(handle, 2.0), 0);
    assert_eq!(processor_add(ptr::null_mut(), 3.0), -1); // null safety
    
    processor_free(handle);
    processor_free(ptr::null_mut()); // safe no-op
    
    println!("All FFI safety tests PASS");
}
```

instruction: Explain and demonstrate panic safety in Rust. Show how to write code that maintains invariants even when panics occur, covering Drop implementations, catch_unwind, and the concept of exception safety.
output:
<think>
Panic safety (also called exception safety) in Rust means ensuring that data structures remain in a valid state even if a panic occurs mid-operation. This matters because: (1) catch_unwind can resume execution after a panic, (2) Drop impls run during unwinding, and (3) data behind Arc/Mutex might be accessed after a panic.

The four levels of exception safety (from C++ but applicable to Rust):
1. No-throw: operation cannot panic (important for Drop impls and FFI)
2. Strong guarantee: operation either succeeds completely or has no effect (transactional)
3. Basic guarantee: invariants are maintained but state may have changed
4. No guarantee: data may be corrupted (the Mutex poisoning scenario)

Rust's standard library generally provides the basic guarantee. Mutex::lock returns a PoisonError if a thread panicked while holding the lock — this is the library detecting a potential invariant violation.

Key patterns for panic safety:
- Do all fallible work before modifying state (strong guarantee)
- Use replace/swap to take ownership before modifying (prevents double-free in Drop)
- Make Drop impls no-panic (double panics abort)
- Use scopeguard or manual cleanup to restore state on panic
- Prefer catch_unwind at boundaries (FFI, thread pools) not for normal control flow

I'll demonstrate each with concrete examples.
</think>

```rust
use std::panic::{self, catch_unwind, AssertUnwindSafe};
use std::sync::{Arc, Mutex};


// === Example 1: Panic-safe data structure modification ===

struct SafeVec<T> {
    data: Vec<T>,
    // Invariant: data is always in a valid state
}

impl<T: Clone> SafeVec<T> {
    fn new() -> Self {
        SafeVec { data: Vec::new() }
    }
    
    /// STRONG guarantee: either all elements are transformed, or none are.
    /// Uses a temporary buffer to ensure atomicity.
    fn transform_all<F>(&mut self, f: F)
    where
        F: Fn(&T) -> T,
    {
        // Do all work on a copy first (strong guarantee)
        let new_data: Vec<T> = self.data.iter().map(|item| f(item)).collect();
        // If f() panics above, self.data is unchanged
        
        // Only swap when all transformations succeeded
        self.data = new_data;
    }
    
    /// BASIC guarantee: some elements may be modified, but Vec is always valid.
    /// More efficient than strong guarantee (no allocation).
    fn transform_in_place<F>(&mut self, f: F)
    where
        F: Fn(&mut T),
    {
        // If f() panics mid-iteration, some elements are modified
        // and some aren't, but the Vec itself is valid
        for item in self.data.iter_mut() {
            f(item);
        }
    }
}


// === Example 2: Drop safety — no panics in Drop ===

struct ResourceGuard {
    name: String,
    cleanup_fn: Option<Box<dyn FnOnce()>>,
}

impl ResourceGuard {
    fn new(name: &str, cleanup: impl FnOnce() + 'static) -> Self {
        ResourceGuard {
            name: name.to_string(),
            cleanup_fn: Some(Box::new(cleanup)),
        }
    }
}

impl Drop for ResourceGuard {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        // CRITICAL: Drop must not panic (double panic = abort)
        // Use take() to move out of Option, avoiding double-drop
        if let Some(cleanup) = self.cleanup_fn.take() {
            // Catch any panic from cleanup to prevent double-panic
            let _ = catch_unwind(AssertUnwindSafe(cleanup));
        }
        // Even if cleanup panicked, our state is valid
        // (cleanup_fn is None, name is valid)
    }
}


// === Example 3: Mutex poisoning and recovery ===

fn mutex_panic_safety() {
    let data = Arc::new(Mutex::new(vec![1, 2, 3]));
    let data_clone = Arc::clone(&data);
    
    // Thread panics while holding the lock
    let handle = std::thread::spawn(move || {
        let mut guard = data_clone.lock().unwrap();
        guard.push(4);
        panic!("oops!"); // Panic while lock is held
        // guard.push(5);  // This never executes
    });
    
    let _ = handle.join(); // Thread panicked
    
    // Mutex is now poisoned — but data is still accessible
    match data.lock() {
        Ok(guard) => println!("Data: {:?}", *guard),
        Err(poison_error) => {
            // We can recover the data if we know it's in a valid state
            let guard = poison_error.into_inner();
            println!("Recovered from poison: {:?}", *guard);
            // Data is [1, 2, 3, 4] — push(4) succeeded, push(5) didn't
        }
    }
}


// === Example 4: Scope guard pattern ===

struct ScopeGuard<F: FnOnce()> {
    callback: Option<F>,
}

impl<F: FnOnce()> ScopeGuard<F> {
    fn new(callback: F) -> Self {
        ScopeGuard { callback: Some(callback) }
    }
    
    /// Disarm the guard (don't run callback on drop).
    fn dismiss(mut self) {
        self.callback = None;
    }
}

impl<F: FnOnce()> Drop for ScopeGuard<F> {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        if let Some(cb) = self.callback.take() {
            cb();
        }
    }
}

fn transactional_operation(data: &mut Vec<i32>) -> Result<(), String> {
    let original_len = data.len();
    
    // Scope guard: on panic, truncate back to original length
    let guard = ScopeGuard::new(|| {
        // This is a simplification — real code would need &mut access
        eprintln!("Rolling back! Original len was {}", original_len);
    });
    
    data.push(10);
    data.push(20);
    // If something panics here, guard runs and logs rollback
    
    guard.dismiss(); // Success — don't roll back
    Ok(())
}


fn main() {
    // Test strong guarantee
    let mut sv = SafeVec::new();
    sv.data = vec![1, 2, 3];
    
    let result = catch_unwind(AssertUnwindSafe(|| {
        sv.transform_all(|x| {
            if *x == 3 { panic!("transform failed!"); }
            x * 2
        });
    }));
    
    assert!(result.is_err());
    assert_eq!(sv.data, vec![1, 2, 3]); // Unchanged! Strong guarantee.
    println!("Strong guarantee: data unchanged after panic");
    
    // Test ResourceGuard
    {
        let _guard = ResourceGuard::new("test", || {
            println!("Cleanup ran safely");
        });
    } // Cleanup runs here
    
    // Test transactional operation
    let mut data = vec![1, 2, 3];
    transactional_operation(&mut data).unwrap();
    assert_eq!(data, vec![1, 2, 3, 10, 20]);
    
    println!("All panic safety tests PASS");
}
```
